story:
It was Saturday, December 9, 2018. I stood at the cemetery with moist eyes, offering flowers at the headstone that read: “Lily DeCruze, May 17, 1959 – June 20, 2018. Your memory lives on.”

Around four years earlier, still single and newly transferred to my company’s manufacturing unit at Verna, Goa, I was commuting from Panjim, where I was lodged at Hotel Fidalgo. The company’s transport made the to and fro manageable, but I needed an affordable place closer to work. One late Saturday evening, on my way to dinner, I stepped into a modest place called Alfred Restobar. Behind it, a small, beautiful villa caught my eye—white walls framed by lush greenery, the leaves shining after an evening rain. While sipping a pre-dinner drink, I casually asked the waiter if there was a decent single room for rent nearby.

Soon a graceful lady appeared beside my table. She regarded me gently, smiled, and asked if I would be interested in a paying guest arrangement. I couldn’t have asked for more and said yes. She nodded and slipped away. The seafood that night was far better than I expected. When I asked for the bill, the waiter returned with a message: Lily wanted to see me at the villa out back. He added quietly that she owned the villa.

He led me along a narrow path to the gate and asked me to wait. A moment later, Lily waved me inside. The living room was tastefully done—warm light, careful touches everywhere. She sat on a sofa and invited me to sit. “I am alone and mostly stay in the master bedroom on the ground floor,” she said, gesturing toward the staircase near the dining area. “There are three bedrooms upstairs, but I rarely use them. My knees don’t let me go up and down.” She hesitated, then added, “I never wanted to let out my ancestral house, but there is something in you… If Alfred hadn’t died in a car accident just before our wedding, we would have had a son like you.” Her voice trailed off.

I understood more than I said. My own parents had died in an air crash when I was still in school. The very next day, I moved in with my bags. From the first week, she treated me like a son—meals, small comforts, that unfailing smile. Whenever I tried to pay, she refused; rent was never accepted. As months passed, age and illness wore her down. The only thing that didn’t change was her charming smile. Like a loving son, I took her to the hospital for checkups, kept track of medicines, and tried to fill the house with conversation.

One morning, when I went in with her bed tea, she didn’t wake. The house turned unbearably quiet.

A couple of days later, a lawyer from Walter and Co. came to the villa. He read out her will: Lily had bequeathed all her property to me. I hadn’t expected it. Truthfully, what I had inherited was a sweet fragrance—her kindness, her faith, her home as a memory of belonging.

Back at the cemetery, I laid lilies on her grave and left, carrying her gentle presence with me. Her memory lives on.
